description.title: Carbon Cap Rule as an automatic cost-containment stabiliser under a persistent cost shock
description.economic_framing: One carbon market hit by a PERSISTENT emissions/cost shock from 2027 onward (BAU jumps from 160 to 198 Mt/yr as both sectors' output rises), which spikes the allowance price. Two otherwise-identical scenarios isolate the mechanism. 'Fixed cap' holds a static 120 Mt cap and lets the shock drive a sustained price spike to 104 USD. 'Carbon Cap Rule' adapts each year's issued permit quantity to the LAGGED deviation of realised aggregate abatement cost (and emissions) from their pre-shock steady-state reference: when abatement costs run hot the regulator issues extra allowances, a soft cost-containment ceiling that damps the sustained spike. NOTE FOR THE ECONOMIST: this is the Benmir-Roman-Taschini Carbon Cap Rule (an adaptive Taylor-rule cap keyed to a one-period-lagged COST deviation), not a spot-price-triggered Cost Containment Reserve (RGGI-style). Its cost-containment behaviour is real but is triggered by the previous year's realised cost crossing its reference, not by the current price crossing a threshold; framing economist-validated (2026-07-12): renamed from the misleading cost-containment-reserve label -- this is the BRT lagged-observable adaptive cap rule (keyed to last year's cost/emissions deviation), NOT a price-triggered RGGI CCR or California APCR that injects reserve volume at a contemporaneous spot-price threshold.
description.citation: Benmir, G., Roman, J. and Taschini, L. (2025), 'Weitzman meets Taylor: EU allowance price drivers and carbon cap rules', Grantham Research Institute Working Paper No. 421, LSE. Mechanism: pe.features.ccr.CCRCapRule (modules/ccr/backend/rules.py, state.py) -- a CapRule on the competitive per-year pipeline reading the previous period's realised aggregates.
description.mechanism: Before clearing, the rule adjusts issued permits by dQ_t = phi_e*(e_{t-1}-ebar)/ebar + phi_z*(z_{t-1}-zbar)/zbar, reading only the PREVIOUS year's realised aggregate emissions e_{t-1} and abatement cost z_{t-1} (never same-year outcomes). Here phi_z = +3.0 Mt (loosen when costs run hot), phi_e = -5.0 Mt (tighten when emissions overshoot), with references set to the 2025-2026 pre-shock steady state: ebar = 120 Mt residual emissions, zbar = 1066.667 aggregate abatement cost (Power 711.11 + Industry 355.56). The extra permits dQ_t are injected as auction supply before competitive clearing, exactly as the MSR withhold/release is.
description.participants: Power E0=100->120 Mt (max abate 60, slope 2) and Industry E0=60->78 Mt (max abate 30, slope 4). Steady BAU 160 Mt/yr (2025-2026); shocked BAU 198 Mt/yr (2027-2029). Against the 120 Mt cap, required abatement jumps from 40 to 78 Mt, lifting the fixed-cap price from 53.33 to 104 USD.

expected_output.reading: The rule is provably neutral before the shock (2025-2027 dQ = 0, references equal the steady state). The shock lands in 2027 and BOTH scenarios spike to 104 USD that year -- the rule cannot react to the impulse year (it only sees 2026's steady cost). From 2028 the lagged cost deviation of +2.80 triggers dQ = +8.41 Mt of extra permits, damping the price to 92.79 USD (11% below the 104 counterfactual); in 2029 the still-elevated cost drives dQ = +5.73 Mt (cost term +6.08 Mt net of a -0.35 Mt emissions-overshoot pullback via phi_e), holding the price at 96.36 USD. The Carbon Cap Rule caps the SUSTAINED phase of the spike below the fixed-cap path, exactly as the paper describes.
